context: Joined because I had to. After 9LOOP, no one wanted me. My career was behind peers who only had work in their life — they do okay because they're not distracted. Joined to redeem myself.
the_good: In the spirit of redeeming myself — no issues here. Did okay.
Everyone loved me except the boss. Don't mind what she thinks.
Made friends here that I plan to keep.
My Nuxt.js architecture was genuinely good — welcomed all feedback, got none, because it was just good.
AI had no problem with it. Things like useApiForm composition for communicating to backend (initial fetch, submit, delete, whatever). Consistent UI designs across pages.
Never missed a deadline. Work had no complaints. Seniors praised my code quality.
The metrics I worked toward: how many pages covered in the revamp migration.
what_went_wrong: Was told it was because 'boss doesn't know if I can do backend' — then was put on backend, did well, still let go.
fair_criticism_of_the_company: Worst code culture I've seen. Senior colleague said it's 3-4/10 in ~15 years of working at various places.
'Hot processor' — not efficient, just hot. Busy doesn't mean productive.
12 of 15 team members have left since I joined. That kind of company.
9 months to decide they don't want me = a sign of a bad company, not a bad employee.
Considering how they treat their employees, it's a sad world if they do okay. They don't look like they're going to do okay.
what_i_worked_on: Nuxt.js frontend revamp — designed the architecture from scratch: composables, consistent UI patterns, reusable API abstractions
The old project had bloated so much they wanted a full revamp. My job: build the foundation, then use it to recreate pages from the old Vue project.
Worked on this for ~6 months. Estimated I needed ~10 months to cover everything + new additions. Boss didn't have the resource.
Was moved to a different project (Laravel backend tasks) for probation assessment.
At least 2 random Laravel tasks — met deadlines, no complaints, seniors loved the work.
Was accidentally over-engineering sometimes — when I saw the mistakes, I fixed them.
lesson_learned: Be better at my job. Be faster. Be better. Still wasn't enough for this company, but the growth was real.
interview_frame: From IMT Tech, I just focused on being better — faster, more reliable. The environment was tough, but I came out a stronger engineer.
original_plan: I always knew I'd eventually leave IMT Tech. The plan was to stay ~2 years (to show loyalty and a cleaner timeline), then leave with Bigcampus ready at my side. The timeline got accelerated by someone else's decision. But Bigcampus is now ready.
notes: ADD MORE MEMORIES HERE AS THEY COME BACK
